name: request
context: Construct and execute HTTP requests with curl — methods, headers, JSON bodies, form data, file uploads, and query parameters
info: This skill covers every aspect of building HTTP requests with curl: choosing the right method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD), setting request headers, sending JSON payloads, posting form data (both URL-encoded and multipart), uploading and downloading files, adding query parameters, following redirects, and handling response output. Agents use this skill whenever they need to interact with a REST or GraphQL API, submit form data, transfer files, or construct complex multi-header requests. The script accepts a URL, method, headers, and body, then executes the curl command and returns the status code, headers, and response body. See [[curl/auth]] for adding authentication to requests and [[curl/debugging]] for troubleshooting failed requests.
triggers: make an http request with curl
send a graphql query with curl
body:
# HTTP Requests with curl

Every curl invocation constructs and sends an HTTP request. This skill covers the full request lifecycle: choosing a method, adding headers, attaching a body, handling response output, and managing redirects.

## Methods

```bash
# GET (default)
curl https://api.example.com/users

# POST — automatically set when using -d
curl -d '{"name":"Alice"}' https://api.example.com/users

# Explicit method override
curl -X PUT -d '{"name":"Bob"}' https://api.example.com/users/1
curl -X PATCH -d '{"email":"new@example.com"}' https://api.example.com/users/1
curl -X DELETE https://api.example.com/users/1

# HEAD — fetch headers only
curl -I https://api.example.com/users
```

## Headers

Use `-H` to set any header. Multiple `-H` flags are allowed.

```bash
curl -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -H "Accept: application/json" \
     -H "X-Request-ID: $(uuidgen)" \
     https://api.example.com/users
```

## Sending JSON

The most common agent pattern — POST or PUT a JSON body:

```bash
# Inline JSON
curl -X POST \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"name": "Alice", "role": "admin"}' \
     https://api.example.com/users

# JSON from a file
curl -X POST \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d @payload.json \
     https://api.example.com/users

# JSON from stdin (pipe from jq)
echo '{"name":"Alice"}' | jq '.role = "admin"' | \
  curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @- https://api.example.com/users

# JSON with shell variable interpolation (use --data-raw to avoid @ interpretation)
curl -X POST \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     --data-raw "{\"name\": \"$USER_NAME\", \"email\": \"$EMAIL\"}" \
     https://api.example.com/users
```

## Form Data

```bash
# URL-encoded form (application/x-www-form-urlencoded)
curl -d "username=alice&password=secret" https://example.com/login

# URL-encode special characters automatically
curl --data-urlencode "query=hello world&special=a+b" https://api.example.com/search

# Multipart form (multipart/form-data) — for file uploads
curl -F "file=@photo.jpg" \
     -F "description=Profile photo" \
     -F "tags=avatar,profile" \
     https://api.example.com/upload

# Multipart with explicit content type
curl -F "data=@report.csv;type=text/csv" https://api.example.com/import
```

## File Transfers

```bash
# Download to specific file
curl -o output.json https://api.example.com/data

# Download using remote filename
curl -LO https://releases.example.com/v1.0/app.tar.gz

# Download with progress bar (default) or silent
curl -sS -o app.tar.gz https://releases.example.com/v1.0/app.tar.gz

# Upload via PUT
curl -T ./backup.sql https://storage.example.com/backups/backup.sql

# Resume interrupted download
curl -C - -O https://releases.example.com/large-file.iso
```

## Query Parameters

```bash
# In the URL directly
curl "https://api.example.com/search?q=curl&limit=10&offset=0"

# Using -G with -d to build query string
curl -G \
     -d "q=curl" \
     -d "limit=10" \
     --data-urlencode "filter=name has spaces" \
     https://api.example.com/search
```

## Redirects

```bash
# Follow redirects (301, 302, 303, 307, 308)
curl -L https://short.url/abc

# Limit redirect hops
curl -L --max-redirs 5 https://short.url/abc

# Follow redirects and send auth to new host (careful!)
curl -L --location-trusted -u user:pass https://api.example.com/resource
```

## Response Handling

```bash
# Save response body and headers separately
curl -D headers.txt -o body.json https://api.example.com/data

# Extract just the status code
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://api.example.com/health

# Extract multiple response fields
curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'status=%{http_code} time=%{time_total}s size=%{size_download}B\n' \
     https://api.example.com/data

# Pipe JSON response to jq for processing
curl -s https://api.example.com/users | jq '.[0].name'

# Fail on HTTP errors (exit code 22 for 4xx/5xx)
curl --fail -s https://api.example.com/data
```

## GraphQL

```bash
# GraphQL query
curl -X POST \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
     -d '{"query": "{ user(id: 42) { name email } }"}' \
     https://api.example.com/graphql

# GraphQL with variables
curl -X POST \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"query": "query($id: ID!) { user(id: $id) { name } }", "variables": {"id": "42"}}' \
     https://api.example.com/graphql
```

## Cookies

```bash
# Send cookies
curl -b "session=abc123; theme=dark" https://example.com/dashboard

# Save cookies from response
curl -c cookies.txt https://example.com/login -d "user=alice&pass=secret"

# Use saved cookies in next request
curl -b cookies.txt https://example.com/dashboard

# Cookie jar round-trip (login then use session)
curl -c jar.txt -d "user=alice&pass=secret" https://example.com/login && \
curl -b jar.txt https://example.com/api/data
```

## Timeout and Retry

```bash
# Connection timeout (time to establish connection)
curl --connect-timeout 5 https://api.example.com/data

# Total operation timeout
curl -m 30 https://api.example.com/slow-endpoint

# Automatic retry with delay
curl --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 --retry-max-time 60 https://api.example.com/data

# Retry only on specific errors (connection refused, timeout)
curl --retry 3 --retry-connrefused https://api.example.com/data
```

sections.title: Sending and receiving JSON
sections.body: Set `Content-Type: application/json` with `-H` and pass the body with `-d`. Read from a file with `-d @file.json` or from stdin with `-d @-`. Use `--data-raw` when your JSON might contain `@` characters that curl would interpret as file references. Always pipe JSON responses through `jq` for parsing. For shell variable interpolation in JSON, prefer building the payload with `jq` rather than string interpolation to avoid escaping issues: `jq -n --arg name "$NAME" '{name: $name}' | curl -d @- -H 'Content-Type: application/json' URL`.

sections.title: Form data and file uploads
sections.body: Use `-d` for URL-encoded forms (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`) — this is what HTML forms submit by default. Use `-F` for multipart forms (`multipart/form-data`) — required for file uploads. With `-F`, prefix file paths with `@`: `-F 'file=@photo.jpg'`. Set the MIME type explicitly with `;type=`: `-F 'file=@data.csv;type=text/csv'`. For literal string values in multipart forms, use `--form-string` to prevent `@` interpretation.

sections.title: Downloading files
sections.body: Use `-o file` to save to a specific path, or `-O` to use the remote filename. Add `-L` to follow redirects (essential for download URLs that redirect through CDNs). Use `-C -` to resume interrupted downloads. For silent downloads in scripts, use `-sS` (silent but show errors). For progress in interactive use, curl shows a progress bar by default. Use `--create-dirs` with `-o` to auto-create parent directories for the output path.

sections.title: Response handling and write-out
sections.body: The `-w` (write-out) flag extracts response metadata using format strings. Key variables: `%{http_code}` (status), `%{time_total}` (total time), `%{time_connect}` (TCP connect time), `%{time_starttransfer}` (time to first byte), `%{size_download}` (body size), `%{content_type}` (MIME type), `%{redirect_url}` (redirect target), `%{num_redirects}` (redirect count). Combine with `-s -o /dev/null` to suppress body output and get just the metadata. Use `-D headers.txt` to dump response headers to a file for inspection.

sections.title: GraphQL requests
sections.body: GraphQL APIs accept POST requests with a JSON body containing `query` and optional `variables` fields. Send with `-X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"query": "...", "variables": {...}}'`. For complex queries, store the GraphQL query in a file and use `jq` to build the payload: `jq -n --rawfile q query.graphql '{query: $q}' | curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d @- URL`. Always include an `Authorization` header — see [[curl/auth]].

scripts.name: HTTP Request
scripts.description: Execute an HTTP request with curl, supporting all methods, headers, body types, and output formatting

CURL_METHOD.description: HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD)

CURL_HEADERS.description: Newline-separated headers, e.g. 'Content-Type: application/json\nAuthorization: Bearer token'

CURL_DATA.description: Request body — raw string, @filename for file, or @- for stdin

CURL_OUTPUT.description: Output mode: body, headers, status, full, or a -w format string

CURL_RETRIES.description: Number of retries on transient failure

CURL_INSECURE.description: Skip SSL verification (true/false) — use only for local dev

body.description: Request body (JSON string, form data, or file path with @ prefix)

ttfb.description: Time to first byte in seconds

depends.reason: curl commands execute in a Bash shell
